clean_story:
Motion as survival. Time stretched or compressed.

The phrase "perceiving speed" doesn’t mean animals wear watches—they experience time differently, like adjusting a camera’s shutter speed. A housefly’s eyes capture 250 flashes per second; yours see 60. To you, a fly swatter moves fast. To the fly, it’s a slow-motion club creeping through molasses.

Speed isn’t just distance over time—it’s how brains process it. Peregrine falcons dive at 240 miles per hour, but their retinas refresh faster than yours, turning blur into crisp frames. A chameleon’s tongue strikes in 0.07 seconds, yet its brain plans the shot in advance, calculating prey motion like a baseball pitcher predicting a swing. Even plants "see" speed: dodder vines grow toward hosts faster when touched repeatedly, as if counting urgency.

Slower creatures stretch time. A star-nosed mole’s brain maps tunnel vibrations in 8 milliseconds—faster than your blink. To it, a worm wriggling underground is a slow, vibrating rope.

Three related subjects are predator reflex arcs, biological time dilation, and how robots mimic animal agility.
